behaviors.description: Webhook requests with invalid HMAC-SHA256 signatures are rejected with 401 status and the body is not forwarded to the queue
behaviors.justification: Signature validation is pure cryptographic logic with no I/O or external dependencies. A unit test with mocked queue can verify (1) invalid signatures cause 401 response and (2) queue.enqueue() is never called. HMAC-SHA256 is deterministic; a mocked queue suffices to verify the conditional skip.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: generate request body with valid HMAC signature, then tamper the body and regenerate signature. Verify that valid signatures pass the check and invalid signatures are rejected with 401 status. Use mock assertions to confirm queue.enqueue() was called exactly once for valid requests and zero times for invalid requests.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test verifies both rejection and non-forwarding at Unit level without real queue access.

behaviors.description: The event is persisted to the queue and that write completes before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender
behaviors.justification: This is about async ordering of side effects in the handler code, not about actual queue persistence. A mocked queue with a controllable promise can verify the handler calls queue.enqueue() and waits for that promise before calling response.send(). The ordering is deterministic and testable without real infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: mock queue.enqueue() to return a delayed promise (e.g., 100ms delay). Capture the order in which queue.enqueue() and response.send() are called. Assert that response.send() is not invoked until the queue promise resolves. Use spy/mock call tracking to verify the ordering constraint.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Ordering of async operations is a code-flow property testable at Unit level; actual persistence is orthogonal.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without blocking on or waiting for downstream event processing to complete
behaviors.justification: This tests whether the handler sends the response without awaiting downstream operations. A unit test with a mocked slow downstream processor (delayed promise) can verify the code path: response.send() must be called/scheduled before awaiting downstream completion. Mocking captures the code structure; the failure would be a blocking await that would be visible in test ordering.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: mock downstream processor to return a 10-second delayed promise. Verify that response.status(200) is called synchronously or scheduled to the event loop before downstream processing completes. Assert via mock spy that response sends before the downstream promise resolves, or measure call timing to show response is not blocked.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The non-blocking property is a code-structure invariant, testable at Unit level without real downstream services.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent requests are being handled
behaviors.justification: Real timing behavior cannot be reliably tested with mocks that complete instantly or return synchronously. The failure boundary is: 'response is delayed beyond 3s under concurrent load'. This depends on actual Node.js event loop scheduling, async task queuing, and concurrent execution. A unit test with mocks would not detect timing regressions (e.g., accidental blocking I/O, inefficient queuing). Must use real async without hiding timing behind instant mocks.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: set up handler with slow-but-realistic mocked downstream (e.g., 100–500ms per event). Send 10+ concurrent webhook requests and measure wall-clock elapsed time from receipt to response sent for each request. Assert that all responses are sent within 3000ms. Verify under realistic concurrent load without needing external services, but with actual Node.js async scheduling.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Timing assurances require real event loop and concurrent async behavior; mocking would give false confidence.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time
behaviors.justification: Deduplication must be backed by persistent state (database, cache, or queue-native tracking) that survives across requests and service restarts. A unit test with an in-memory mock would verify the code logic but not catch failures in the persistence mechanism itself (e.g., state lost on restart, database query error, race condition in state lookup). To verify deduplication actually works, we must test against real persistent storage.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: (1) send a webhook with event ID 'evt-123' and verify it is enqueued, (2) send an identical webhook with the same ID and verify the handler returns 200 but the event is not enqueued again, (3) query the real queue/database and assert exactly one event with ID 'evt-123' is present. Test across service boundaries to ensure state persists.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Logic can be unit-tested, but actual deduplication enforcement requires Integration-level real persistence layer testing.

behaviors.description: When the persistent queue reports it is full and rejects the event, the handler returns HTTP 503 Service Unavailable with a Retry-After header
behaviors.justification: This tests the handler's error-handling code path: catch a 'queue full' error from queue.enqueue() and respond with 503 + Retry-After. The logic is in the handler, not in the queue. A mocked queue that throws the 'full' error can verify the handler's response. We are not testing whether the queue actually enforces capacity (that is Behavior 7).
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: mock queue.enqueue() to throw an error with code/message indicating capacity exceeded (e.g., 'queue-full' or 'ENOSPC'). Call the handler with this error thrown. Assert that the response status is 503 and includes a Retry-After header with a reasonable value (e.g., 60 seconds).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Tests handler's error response path; the queue's capacity enforcement is verified separately in Behavior 7.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed the limit
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests the queue's own enforcement mechanism, not the handler's response. A mocked queue would completely hide the failure (mocks never enforce limits). To verify real capacity enforcement, a real queue instance is required. Failure modes include: misconfigured capacity limit, ineffective enforcement, race conditions allowing overflow. All require actual queue infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: configure the persistent queue with a low capacity limit (e.g., 10 events max). Using the queue client directly (not through the handler), enqueue events until full. Attempt one more enqueue and verify it fails/is rejected. Confirm the queue actually rejected the write, not a mock. Use a real message queue (RabbitMQ, Redis, or in-memory test instance with enforced limits) to verify the enforcement logic itself.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Verifies the queue's own enforcement, not the handler's reaction; requires real queue to detect configuration or enforcement bugs.
